item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Untranslated spanish, without a footnote explaining what it means, seems pretty un-foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For all the non-Abrahamic religious SCPs on this site, it was an interesting break from the norm to read one about the Virgin Mary. And a damn good one at that.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really a very good work, well done. Although the anomaly itself is inconspicuous, the story and the message it gives manage to reach the reader.

+1

…and Juan Carlos de Palencia received what he deserved

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If i read this correctly (i cant really tell tbh), Cristobal used his own heart as "the heart of the cruel"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of the most hopeful skips out there. I thought the witchcraft would backfire on him badly but apparently it worked in his favor and in favor of everyone else like him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm usually not a huge fan of religious scips but I really loved this. The story and characters are incredibly compelling. Out of the contest articles I've read so far, I feel this one fits the theme the best as well.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A very moving and intimate piece. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 from me. This was a beautiful read, very visceral. I hope Cristobal is at peace.

item.title: Touched My Soul
item.content:encoded:
Character, Message, Story, Culture.

This entry does all four fantastically, where I tend to see others fail at through focusing at one too much. Great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You really don't lose time diving from the brief description into the meat of the article, which is a highly immersive and wonderfully characterized series of journals and excerpts. The build-up to the final conflict between the demon and the Virgin Mary has wonderful pacing and really makes this a fast read.

I'm not the biggest fan of the note at the end, as it kind of kills the immersion for me and ends the article on a "be careful or this kind of thing will happen to us" note which, in my opinion, has been done to death before, and in far better execution than it is done here. Still, the rest of the article before that more than makes up for this falter.

+1. Excellent work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's something about this one that really resonated with me. I'm not particularly religious or spiritual, but the story behind this one was very well written and full of feelings. Very much enjoyed it, and I like how *different* it is to most articles.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to second this; as documents pertaining to an anomaly, there was no question in my mind as to why they would be translated into the primary language used by the Foundation.

And yeah, echoing above. Most of this was really great, but the note at the end really killed it for me. Above all the other comments, it just makes no sense to me that something like that would be attached to an SCP document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this one. It has a keen sense of history and religion and tells a very compelling story. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the name feedback! I've updated accordingly.

I'm definitely aware of the issues you've brought up, and it's especially important that I address them because the characterization and narrative are the backbone of the article. I'll see what I can do!

And yeah, I know it's really unlikely that Cristóbal would even have been literate, let alone been able to keep a journal… I'm afraid I'm just going to have to call "suspension of disbelief" on that one.

Thanks for the detailed feedback! I really appreciate it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Cristobál Bazán and Luis de Riviera?
- hide

I’m Spanish, can assure that our grammatical rules forbid putting an accent in the last syllable of a word when it ends on a consonant other than n or s. You probably meant Cristóbal, which was a fairly common name in those times (i.e. see how we write Christopher Columbus).

On the other hand, the particle de in surnames was only used by nobility and renowned individuals. Is there any slave with the surname “de Riviera” in the article you’ve linked? That’d be strange. If that’s not the case, I suggest to change his surname for a plain “Riviera” or any other that sounds good.

Well, this is a piece worth reading, but I don’t think it reaches its true potential. It talks about humanity…, but its character lack it. The characterization is so basic that perceiving them as persons is really difficult. Cristóbal, the most defined character, can be described in just three traits: he’s afraid of beatings, he’s devout, and he is in love with Catalina.

And that’s it. There’s absolutely nothing else about him; I don’t know his personality, I can’t even imagine how he’ll react to most situations, the thoughts and impressions that he writes are barely a few and don’t give insight of his head… I’m supposed to put in the shoes of someone that I don’t know at all. And it’s hard to see something so vague and ambiguous as a person. By expanding a little of his personality and thoughts (and perhaps of other characters), you could make the journals more relatable and communicate in a less explicit fashion the message of the article: these people were persons with dreams, thoughts, feelings, family, lives; they deserved freedom and safety to experience all this things as they deserved; their oppression was damnable and everyone must ensure it doesn’t happen again.

Ahem. The journal entries as a narrative resource are a bit hackneyed, but I can’t really complain; in this one was necessary. But…, how’s that a slave has a steady supply of paper and ink? He doesn’t make inventory, he shouldn’t even know how to write. :P

These two details keep me from voting positive. However, this isn’t bad, and it’d be unfair if I voted negative. Neutral for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it helps, I never questioned why those journals would have been translated in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're all spot-on. The tone and content of the final note was by far the biggest stumbling-block in the process, and I also feel dissatisfied with it. I needed to make the connection at the end, and I wanted to have an in-universe reason why someone would go to the effort of translating supplemental materials for such a simple anomaly. Maybe I'll make some edits if I can think of a better way to do it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel as if the note exists because of the connection between the character writing it and another character in the story. I'm not a fan of "We need to use this SCP as a moral" endings, but I can see why in-universe, the author of the note would have felt some reason (and pride) over it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to agree with the assessment of the final note. I couldn't quite put my finger on it at the time, but that was definitely bothering me. I think the article would be stronger without it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My biggest issue with the piece is its format. I’ve grown jaded to journal entries by themselves and I dislike the note at the end hammering in the fairly non-subtle themes of the article for any readers who failed to pick up on them initially.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A totally valid critique! I definitely see how most winning articles have some sort of grand cosmic significance for the Foundation, and I wanted to try to make the anomaly significant on a deeper, more philosophical level.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! If you're able to get past the paywall somehow, read the article it's based off. Gripping, fascinating stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you like it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same here, it's very engaging and the characters all have some depth to them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The writing and story are fantastic, but I can't shake the feeling that the anomaly itself isn't all that interesting. Will likely upvote after the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The history this article is steeped in is very well realized. Fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So very good! I wish I had the cognitive function to write more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks so much for your feedback! This wouldn’t have been what it is without your help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: High praise from someone as experienced/well-known as you! Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
there is only one interest and it IS religion

thank u so much!! glad you like it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thank u and god bless

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Already my favorite 4000 entry that I've read from draft form + those posted on the site. Excellent pacing and characterization.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is so good. this kicks ass.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i read this and immediately became gatholic good work ✝1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Advice taken! Thanks for the tip! And I'm glad you like it; it was a blast to write! Yours is amazing as well! One of my favorite format screws in recent history.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been waiting for you to post this. It's my favorite article I've seen so far. This is truly something beautiful. This one is like one of those entries where I just wanna shake the writer's hand for writing it.

It's good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It got a little hard to read in the middle (the transition to a new journal was a bit jarring and I didn't notice at first); I'd recommend putting a horizontal line between journals written by different people to act as a clear barrier to morons like myself.

Beyond that, this is amazing. Like, really. Magic and spirituality among slaves isn't something that's really ever explored in fiction, you justify why some parts are translated and others aren't, and the implication that the researcher at the end is related to the Inquisitor opens up great story possibilities. +1, and good luck in the contest.

E: Also I just realized, from the description of the corpse… is it immaculate? Are you implying that Bazán somehow achieved sainthood? That kind of makes me like it more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A story about the unfathomable violence of colonialism and the unstoppable power of love.

Thank you to stormbreath, Golden506, kinchtheknifeblade, and Sooperrandomname for their critiques.

Image is CC from here.

Story and title were inspired by Javier Villa Flores, To Lose One's Soul: Blasphemy and Slavery in New Spain, 1596-1669.

Good luck to everyone!

Edit 7/7: I have removed the note at the end. Most folks were saying it was too ham-fisted, and I'm beginning to agree. Thank you for your feedback!
